steps.prompt: A sail leans — so what counts as its height?

graphic.name: sail
graphic.caption: A triangular sail with a slanted edge. To find its area you use the height measured straight down from the top — the dashed orange line — not the length of the slanted edge.

steps.body:
Area counts how many unit squares fit inside a shape, and almost every area grows from one idea: [base](blue) × [height](orange).
The trick is the [height](orange). It is measured straight across, perpendicular to the [base](blue) — like a plumb line dropped from the top corner. It is NOT the length of the [slanted side](slate), which leans and is always a bit longer.

Every formula traces back to [base](blue) × [height](orange):
Parallelogram = [b](blue) × [h](orange).  Triangle = ½ × [b](blue) × [h](orange), half a parallelogram.  Trapezoid = ½ × ([b₁](blue) + [b₂](blue)) × [h](orange), the average of the two parallel sides times the height.  Kite = ½ × d₁ × d₂, half the box around its diagonals.  Equilateral triangle of side s = (√3 ÷ 4) × s².
In every one, the [height](orange) is the straight-down (perpendicular) distance — never the slant.

steps.prompt: Slide the corner. Watch the area hold.
steps.subtitle: Drag the top corner sideways: the shape leans, but the [base](blue) and the [height](orange) (the dashed orange plumb line) don't change — so the area stays put. Raise or lower the corner and the [height](orange) changes, and the area follows it.
This parallelogram has [base](blue) 10 and a straight-down [height](orange) of 5. Its [slanted side](slate) is 6. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: The base is 10, and the slanted side is 6.
Area of a parallelogram = base x height.

steps.explanation: Line 3 used the [slanted side](slate) (6) in place of the [height](orange). The height is the straight-down distance to the [base](blue) — here it is 5, not 6. The real area is [base](blue) × [height](orange) = 10 × 5 = 50. The slant always overshoots because it leans.
steps.hint: Which length drops straight down to the base — the slanted side, or the dashed orange height?
steps.doneNote: Exactly — the slant is not the height.

steps.subtitle: Every claim is about the area formulas. Each one comes back to [base](blue) × [height](orange) — and the [height](orange) is the straight-down distance, never the [slant](slate).

cards.statement: A triangle's area is ½ × base × height.
cards.note: Yes — a triangle is half a parallelogram, so ½ × b × h.

cards.statement: A parallelogram's area is base × its slanted side.
cards.note: No — it's base × perpendicular height. The slant leans and overshoots: for base 10, height 5, slant 6, the area is 10 × 5 = 50, not 10 × 6.

cards.statement: A trapezoid's area is the average of its two parallel sides times the height.
cards.note: Yes — ½ × (b₁ + b₂) × h is exactly the average of the parallel sides, times the height.

cards.statement: A kite's area is ½ × d₁ × d₂, half the product of its diagonals.
cards.note: Yes — it's half the box drawn around its two diagonals.

cards.statement: A parallelogram with base 10 and height 5 has an area of 60.
cards.note: No — base × height = 10 × 5 = 50. The 60 sneaks in the slanted side (6) where the height belongs.

steps.doneNote: Triangle, trapezoid and kite each carry a ½; the parallelogram is the full base × height — and the height is always the straight-down distance.

steps.prompt: Proof: a parallelogram is a rectangle in disguise

gates.prompt: To turn the parallelogram into a shape we already know, what should we draw?

choices.label: The [height](orange) — a straight-down line from a top corner to the [base](blue).

gates.hint: Cut straight down along the perpendicular height.

gates.prompt: We slide the sliced-off right triangle to the other end. Why does it fit exactly?

choices.label: Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal.

gates.hint: The two slanted sides are equal and parallel.

gates.prompt: Now it's a rectangle with the same [base](blue) and [height](orange). Its area is…

choices.label: [base](blue) × [height](orange) — and half of it, a triangle, is ½ × b × h.

gates.hint: Rectangle area is base × height; the base and height never changed.

stages: Start with a parallelogram. Its [base](blue) and its straight-down [height](orange) are what the area depends on.
Drop the [height](orange) straight down from a top corner to the [base](blue). It slices a right triangle off one end.
Slide that triangle across to the other end. Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal, so it fits perfectly.
What's left is a rectangle with the same [base](blue) and [height](orange), so the area is [b](blue) × [h](orange) — and a triangle, half of one, is ½ × b × h.

steps.subtitle: Work out each area with [base](blue) × [height](orange) (and a ½ where the formula has one), then line them up. Every one uses the straight-down [height](orange), never the slant.
steps.instruction: Tap them from smallest area to largest.

items.label: Trapezoid: parallel sides 9 and 5, height 4  →  ½ × (9 + 5) × 4 = 28

steps.doneNote: 20 < 28 < 42 < 60. Triangle, trapezoid, parallelogram, kite — each from base × height.

problems.prompt: A triangle has base 8 and perpendicular height 5. Find its area.

feedback.incorrect: A triangle's area is ½ × base × height — use the straight-down height.

problems.prompt: A parallelogram has base 7 and perpendicular height 6. Find its area.

feedback.incorrect: A parallelogram's area is base × height (the straight-down height).

problems.prompt: A trapezoid has parallel sides 9 and 5 and a perpendicular height of 4. Find its area.

feedback.incorrect: Average the two parallel sides, then multiply by the height: ½ × (b₁ + b₂) × h.

problems.strongHint: Add the parallel sides first: ½ × (9 + 5) × 4.
